title: Arancini al ragù
subtitle: Sicilian fried rice balls with meat ragù and peas
excerpt: Rice cooked with saffron, formed around a filling of meat ragù and peas, coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until the outside shatters and the inside is creamy and hot. The quintessential Sicilian street food, and one of the great fried things. Takes time but is not technically difficult.
sourceNotes: Arancini are documented as a Sicilian preparation from at least the tenth century, with Arabic culinary influence apparent in the saffron-rice base. The name means little oranges, from the colour and shape of the saffron-tinted balls. They are documented in Sicilian regional cooking literature from the nineteenth century and appear in their modern form (with ragù and peas) in twentieth-century Sicilian cookbooks. They are sold as street food at friggitorie and bakeries throughout Sicily, particularly in Palermo and Catania.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze coated, unfried arancini. Fry from frozen at 160°C for 8 to 10 minutes until heated through and golden.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch and freeze uncooked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rice and ragù can be made a day ahead. Shape and coat just before frying.

content.text: Arancini are a project. They are not a quick dinner. But they reward the effort with something that is very good indeed: the shattering crust, the dense, golden rice, the hot meat filling. They can be made in stages over two days without any single stage being onerous. The rice goes first; it needs to cool completely. The ragù is simple and quick. The shaping and coating takes 30 minutes. The frying is done in batches.

The saffron is not optional. It gives the rice its orange colour and a faint floral bitterness that contrasts with the rich ragù inside. Without it, arancini are just fried rice balls; with it, they are something that has been made in Sicily for over a thousand years.

items.prepNote: 1 beaten into the rice; 2 beaten for coating

content.text: Cook the {{arborio-rice}} of risotto rice using the absorption method: combine with the {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock and the dissolved {{saffron}} in a saucepan, bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 18 minutes until the rice is tender and has absorbed the liquid. Off the heat, stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{parmesan}} of Parmesan. Beat in one egg. Season well with salt. Spread onto a tray and leave to cool completely — at least 1 hour, or overnight in the fridge.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a pan over medium heat. Cook the {{onion}} onion for 5 minutes. Add the {{beef-mince}} and cook, breaking it up, until browned. Pour in the {{red-wine}} of wine and let bubble for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes. Simmer for 15 minutes until thick. Stir in the {{peas-frozen}} of peas and cook for 2 minutes. Season well. Cool completely.

Set up a coating station: {{plain-flour}} in one bowl, the 2 beaten eggs in a second, and the {{breadcrumbs-dried}} in a third. Take a palm-sized amount of cold rice and flatten it in your hand. Place a heaped teaspoon of ragù in the centre. Close the rice around it, pressing firmly to seal, and shape into a cone or ball. Roll in flour, then egg, then breadcrumbs, pressing firmly to adhere. Place on a lined tray and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a deep pot to 170°C. Fry the arancini in batches of 3 to 4 for 4 to 5 minutes, turning once, until deep golden all over. Drain on kitchen paper. Eat hot.

items.cause: Rice not cold enough; filling too wet; not pressed firmly enough when shaped
items.fix: Make sure rice is completely cold before shaping; dry the ragù so it is thick before filling; press firmly when closing

items.symptom: Coating falls off in the oil
items.cause: Flour step skipped; arancini not refrigerated after coating
items.fix: Flour before egg before breadcrumbs, in that order; refrigerate the coated arancini for 30 minutes before frying

items.symptom: Inside is cold and the rice tastes raw
items.cause: Oil too hot; outside cooked before heat reached the centre
items.fix: Fry at 170°C rather than higher; for large arancini, reduce to 160°C and cook for 6 minutes

items.symptom: Rice mixture is too sticky to shape
items.cause: Rice not cooled enough; or ratio of butter to rice too high
items.fix: Cool completely in the fridge for at least 1 hour before shaping; wet hands help when moulding

content.text: Arancini are the street food of Sicily: sold from friggitorie, bakeries, and bars throughout the island, eaten standing at a counter with a napkin, hot from the oil. They are morning food in Sicily, served for breakfast alongside a granita and a brioche. The name means little oranges, from the golden, round shape that resembles the Sicilian blood orange. The saffron comes from the Arab culinary influence that shaped Sicilian cooking between the ninth and twelfth centuries, leaving behind a spice tradition that survives in arancini, couscous (in the Trapani region), and the sweet-sour agrodolce preparations found throughout the island.
